content_html:
<html:div xmlns:html="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml">
<html:p class="ai-disclaimer">Written by me, proof-read by an LLM.
<html:br />Details at end.</html:p>

<html:p>Sometimes the compiler decides the best way to optimise your loop is to... write it twice. Sounds counterintuitive? Let's change our <html:code>sum</html:code> <html:a href="/202512/10-loop-unrolling">example from before</html:a> to optionally return a sum-of-squares<html:sup id="fnref:armagain"><html:a class="footnote-ref" href="#fn:armagain">1</html:a></html:sup>:</html:p>

<html:p>At <html:code>-O2</html:code> the compiler turns the ternary into: <html:code>sum += value * (squared ? value : 1);</html:code> - using a multiply and add (<html:code>mla</html:code>) instruction to do the multiply and add, and conditionally picking either <html:code>value</html:code> or the constant <html:code>1</html:code> to avoid a branch inside the loop.</html:p>

<html:p>However, if we turn the optimisation level up, the compiler uses a new approach:</html:p>

<html:p>Here the compiler realises the <html:code>bool squared</html:code> value doesn't change throughout the loop, and decides to duplicate the loop: one copy that squares each time unconditionally, and one where it never multiplies at all. This is called "loop unswitching".</html:p>

<html:p>The check of <html:code>squared</html:code> is moved out of the loop, and the appropriate loop is then selected, either <html:code>.LBB0_4</html:code> (non-squaring) or continuing to <html:code>.LBB0_2</html:code> (the squaring version).</html:p>

<html:p>Each loop is perfectly optimised for its duties, and it's as if you had written:</html:p>

<html:p>By duplicating the loop this way, the compiler makes sure that the multiplication doesn't happen unless you specifically asked for it. In the <html:code>-O2</html:code> code, the compiler bet on doing the multiply-and-add each time<html:sup id="fnref:bpu"><html:a class="footnote-ref" href="#fn:bpu">2</html:a></html:sup> even when it wasn't needed. In the loop unswitching case, we do pay a code size penalty (<html:em>some</html:em> code is duplicated, after all), and that's why loop unswitching didn't occur on the lower optimisation setting.</html:p>

<html:p>As always, it's good to trust your compiler's decisions, but know the kinds of trade-offs it's making at various optimisation levels. You can always verify what it's doing with <html:a href="https://godbolt.org">Compiler Explorer</html:a>, after all!</html:p>

<html:p><html:em>See <html:a href="https://youtu.be/-VCrYshE7iQ">the video</html:a> that accompanies this post.</html:em></html:p>

<html:hr />

<html:p><html:em>This post is day 12 of <html:a href="/AoCO2025-archive">Advent of Compiler Optimisations 2025</html:a>,
a 25-day series exploring how compilers transform our code.</html:em></html:p>

<html:p><html:em>← <html:a href="/202512/11-pop-goes-the-weasel-er-count">Pop goes the...population count?</html:a> | <html:a href="/202512/13-licking-licm">Loop-Invariant Code Motion</html:a> →</html:em></html:p>

<html:p><html:em>This post was written by a human (<html:a href="/MattGodbolt">Matt Godbolt</html:a>) and reviewed and proof-read by LLMs and humans.</html:em></html:p>

<html:p><html:em>Support Compiler Explorer on <html:a href="https://patreon.com/c/mattgodbolt">Patreon</html:a>
or <html:a href="https://github.com/sponsors/compiler-explorer">GitHub</html:a>,
or by buying CE products in the <html:a href="https://shop.compiler-explorer.com">Compiler Explorer Shop</html:a></html:em>.</html:p>

<html:div class="footnote">
<html:hr />
<html:ol>
<html:li id="fn:armagain">
<html:p>We're using ARMv7 again here for simplicity and to yet again avoid vectorisation that we'll get to later in this series. <html:a class="footnote-backref" href="#fnref:armagain" title="Jump back to footnote 1 in the text">↩</html:a></html:p>
</html:li>
<html:li id="fn:bpu">
<html:p>In this case the compiler determined that just doing the multiply every time would be cheaper than a branch on every iteration to avoid it. Branch predictors do help this in general, but it's still better to do one branch at the beginning and specialise each loop individually. <html:a class="footnote-backref" href="#fnref:bpu" title="Jump back to footnote 2 in the text">↩</html:a></html:p>
</html:li>
</html:ol>
</html:div>

    </html:div>
content_text: Written by me, proof-read by an LLM. Details at end. Sometimes the compiler decides the best way to optimise your loop is to... write it twice. Sounds counterintuitive? Let's change our sum example from before to optionally return a sum-of-squares 1 : At -O2 the compiler turns the ternary into: sum += value * (squared ? value : 1); - using a multiply and add ( mla ) instruction to do the multiply and add, and conditionally picking either value or the constant 1 to avoid a branch inside the loop. However, if we turn the optimisation level up, the compiler uses a new approach: Here the compiler realises the bool squared value doesn't change throughout the loop, and decides to duplicate the loop: one copy that squares each time unconditionally, and one where it never multiplies at all. This is called "loop unswitching". The check of squared is moved out of the loop, and the appropriate loop is then selected, either .LBB0_4 (non-squaring) or continuing to .LBB0_2 (the squaring version). Each loop is perfectly optimised for its duties, and it's as if you had written: By duplicating the loop this way, the compiler makes sure that the multiplication doesn't happen unless you specifically asked for it. In the -O2 code, the compiler bet on doing the multiply-and-add each time 2 even when it wasn't needed. In the loop unswitching case, we do pay a code size penalty ( some code is duplicated, after all), and that's why loop unswitching didn't occur on the lower optimisation setting. As always, it's good to trust your compiler's decisions, but know the kinds of trade-offs it's making at various optimisation levels. You can always verify what it's doing with Compiler Explorer , after all! See the video that accompanies this post. This post is day 12 of Advent of Compiler Optimisations 2025 , a 25-day series exploring how compilers transform our code. ← Pop goes the...population count? | Loop-Invariant Code Motion → This post was written by a human ( Matt Godbolt ) and reviewed and proof-read by LLMs and humans. Support Compiler Explorer on Patreon or GitHub , or by buying CE products in the Compiler Explorer Shop . We're using ARMv7 again here for simplicity and to yet again avoid vectorisation that we'll get to later in this series. ↩ In this case the compiler determined that just doing the multiply every time would be cheaper than a branch on every iteration to avoid it. Branch predictors do help this in general, but it's still better to do one branch at the beginning and specialise each loop individually. ↩
summary: Duplicating loops around can yield some decent optimisations
title: Unswitching loops for fun and profit
